nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich seaward reefs, typically at depths of 6–25 m but reported as deep as 80 m; adults forage actively over sandy patches between reef structure.

diet.notes: Feeds on snails, worms, mussels, small crustaceans, and small fish. In the wild, often forages over sandy areas by blowing water across the substrate to uncover hidden prey. Generally accepts a range of frozen meaty foods (mysis, krill, brine shrimp, chopped shellfish) in captivity.

summary: An aggressive Indo-Pacific wrasse with a reddish-orange body and white spots near the dorsal fin. Generally considered an active swimmer and predator of small invertebrates, and is not commonly considered reef safe.
careNotes:
The Red Diana Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level marine species — hardy and disease-resistant, but with a reputation for being one of the more aggressive members of the Bodianus genus. It has been known to nip at bivalves, clams, ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, worms, and small fish, so is commonly considered incompatible with most reef invertebrates. Reports vary on coral nipping; ReefApp lists it 'reef safe with caution', while LiveAquaria lists it as not reef safe.

This species tends to be a strong swimmer and active forager, often searching the substrate by blowing jets of water across sand to uncover hidden prey. It generally benefits from a long aquarium with ample open swim space combined with rocky hiding places and overhangs to retreat into.

Length disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria reports a maximum of 10 in (25.4 cm), FishBase records 16.9 cm SL (standard length, ~20–22 cm total length), ReefApp reports 17 cm, and AquaticCommunity reports 9.8 in (25 cm). A range of 17–25 cm reflects the spread.

Tank size disagreement: LiveAquaria recommends 150 gal (≈568 L), ReefApp recommends 500 L (132 gal), and AquaticCommunity recommends 125 gal (≈475 L). Given the fish's active hunting behavior and aggressive temperament, 500 L was selected as a cautious round-number value that accommodates all three recommendations.

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase records wild habitat temperatures of 24–28°C. Averaged across sources: approximately 23–27°C.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. As a labrid, this species is generally believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. Captive breeding has not been reliably documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product care sheet. Listed minimum tank as 150 gal (≈568 L), temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, care level moderate, temperament aggressive, not reef safe. Note: the collection-page URL supplied by the discovery agent was just a catalog index; this product-page URL is the actual care sheet.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria), adult size (16.9 cm SL), depth range (6–80 m), native range (Indian Ocean from East Africa to Cocos-Keeling Islands), and habitat temperature 24–28°C. Temperature range differs from LiveAquaria (22.2–25.6°C); averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult length (17 cm), reef safe rating ('with caution'), tank volume (500 L / ≈132 gal), behavior (sand-foraging, hermaphroditic). Slightly more conservative reef-safe rating than LiveAquaria's 'No'.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: max size 9.8 in (25 cm), minimum 125 gal, aggressive, not reef safe, carnivore (mysis/brine and frozen meaty foods), Indo-west-Pacific range.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing behavior in the wild.
